topics.headline: California May Revise signals flat categorical growth — Title III becomes the lever for LTEL services
topics.why_matters: A+ has 5 active partners with confirmed Title III Immigrant receipts (Ocean Grove, South Sutter, Visions, Heartland, Pacific Coast Academy) and 18 partners that have never applied. When LCFF growth flattens, the question for special programs coordinators becomes "what categorical funds am I leaving on the table?" Danielle's existing pitch angle is now backed by the budget cycle itself.
topics.angle: "Three Title III line items most NCB charters are underutilizing — and how to deploy them before the June obligation window."
topics.roman_take: Argue that the May Revise exposes a structural problem — California built its EL accountability system faster than it built the funding mechanisms to serve LTELs, and charter schools are stuck closing the gap with categorical scraps.
topics.danielle_take: Walk through the exact Title III Immigrant unspent-funds problem ($7,384 sitting at Community Collaborative, $3,813 at Cabrillo Point) and what a coordinator can do in the next 30 days to deploy or risk losing it.

topics.headline: The post-ESSER vendor shakeout — who's still standing and why it matters for charter directors planning FY26–27
topics.why_matters: Charter directors are right now writing FY26–27 intervention plans and getting cold-called by vendors who can't credibly say whether they'll exist in 18 months. A+ has operated continuously through ESSER ramp-up AND ESSER cliff because the funding model was never ESSER-dependent — it's Title I, Title III, and LCFF. This is the most defensible differentiation A+ has, and almost no one in the space is naming it out loud.
topics.angle: "Three questions every charter director should ask a tutoring vendor before signing a FY26–27 contract" — frame around funding-source durability, not pedagogy.
topics.roman_take: The ESSER-funded tutoring industry built a Ponzi scheme of scale on a deadline nobody wanted to look at. The companies imploding now told districts they were "high-dosage" when they were actually high-headcount and low-evidence.
topics.danielle_take: When a district loses a tutoring vendor mid-year, the kids who suffer first are LTELs and IEP-served students because their service plans assume continuity. Here's what a continuity-first vendor evaluation rubric actually looks like. ---
